examples.title: Basic Raster Scan Pattern Simulation
examples.description: Simulates how a raster scan display works by scanning line by line from top to bottom, filling pixels row by row - like traditional CRT monitors
examples.code:
import time

def raster_scan_simulation(width, height):
    """
    Simulates raster scan display behavior.
    In raster scan, electron beam sweeps across the screen
    from left to right, then moves to next line (top to bottom).
    """
    frame_buffer = [[0 for _ in range(width)] for _ in range(height)]
    
    scan_pattern = []
    
    # Raster scan: top-left to bottom-right, row by row
    for row in range(height):
        for col in range(width):
            frame_buffer[row][col] = 1  # Pixel illuminated
            position = (row, col)
            scan_pattern.append(position)
            
            # Simulate refresh rate delay
            if len(scan_pattern) % width == 0:
                print(f"Completed row {row + 1}/{height}")
    
    return scan_pattern

# Example: Simulate 10x5 raster scan
width, height = 10, 5
pattern = raster_scan_simulation(width, height)
print(f"\nTotal pixels scanned: {len(pattern)}")
print(f"Scan order: {pattern[:5]}... (first 5 positions)")
examples.explanation: This code simulates the raster scan process where: 1) The electron beam starts at top-left corner 2) Sweeps horizontally across each row 3) Moves down to next row 4) Repeats until bottom-right. This is how traditional CRT monitors work, drawing pixels in a predictable sequential pattern rather than random access.

examples.title: Random Scan (Vector) Line Drawing
examples.description: Demonstrates random scan (vector) display behavior - drawing lines directly from any point to any point in random order, not constrained to row-by-row scanning
examples.code:
import math

class RandomScanDisplay:
    """
    Random Scan / Vector Display simulation.
    Unlike raster, vector displays draw lines directly between
    any two points, like an oscilloscope or pen plotter.
    """
    
    def __init__(self, width, height):
        self.width = width
        self.height = height
        self.beam_position = (width // 2, height // 2)
        self.vector_list = []
    
    def move_beam(self, x, y):
        """Move electron beam to new position (random access)"""
        start = self.beam_position
        end = (x, y)
        self.beam_position = end
        self.vector_list.append((start, end))
        return start, end
    
    def draw_line(self, x1, y1, x2, y2):
        """Draw line between any two points (Bresenham's algorithm)"""
        points = []
        dx = abs(x2 - x1)
        dy = abs(y2 - y1)
        sx = 1 if x1 < x2 else -1
        sy = 1 if y1 < y2 else -1
        err = dx - dy
        
        x, y = x1, y1
        while True:
            points.append((x, y))
            if x == x2 and y == y2:
                break
            e2 = 2 * err
            if e2 > -dy:
                err -= dy
                x += sx
            if e2 < dx:
                err += dx
                y += sy
        
        self.vector_list.append(((x1, y1), (x2, y2)))
        return points

# Demo: Draw shapes using random scan (vector) approach
display = RandomScanDisplay(100, 100)

# Draw a square - each side drawn independently, any order
square_lines = [
    display.draw_line(10, 10, 90, 10),   # Top
    display.draw_line(90, 10, 90, 90),   # Right
    display.draw_line(90, 90, 10, 90),   # Bottom
    display.draw_line(10, 90, 10, 10),   # Left
]

# Draw diagonal - random access from anywhere
display.move_beam(10, 10)
display.draw_line(10, 10, 90, 90)

print(f"Total vectors drawn: {len(display.vector_list)}")
print(f"Vector display can draw from ANY point to ANY point!")
print("Unlike raster, no fixed scan order required.")
examples.explanation: This demonstrates random scan (vector) displays: 1) The beam can move directly to any position 2) Lines are drawn between any two points 3) Order is arbitrary/random 4) Used in oscilloscopes, vector graphics, early CRT displays 5) Unlike raster, there's no row-by-row constraint - true random access

examples.title: Frame Buffer Implementation for Raster Display
examples.description: Advanced example showing a complete frame buffer system - the core memory that stores pixel data for raster displays, with pixel-level operations
examples.explanation: This advanced example shows the fundamental concept behind all modern raster displays: 1) Frame buffer is dedicated memory storing every pixel's color 2) Each pixel has specific memory location (random access within scan constraints) 3) Operations include set_pixel, get_pixel, clear 4) Raster displays must scan entire buffer row by row to display 5) This is exactly how GPUs work - render to frame buffer, then display controller scans it out
examples.timeComplexity: O(width × height) for rendering, O(1) for pixel operations
